author: @jezlawrence720
text:
Hang on so why aren't these fitted in every house and connected up so whenever you run the taps you reduce your grid consumption?

Implemented nationwide in uk how much would that reduce grid power demands? Surely even a fraction would be good?

Mind you, you'd need some way to stop people just turning on their taps and leaving them that way I suppose.

author: @bettyswallocks6411
text: What about using the discs from mechanical hard disk drives? Back in the day, the exchangeable cartridges had several platters that were 40cm or more in diameter, but more recent 5.25 HDDs should be good for your experimental needs, and there must be plenty available these days - I have 4 x 500gb drives in a box somewhere. I think I’d better dig them out.

author: @al3xf103
text: Very cool, even more so that it was first invented over 100 years ago! Imagine going back in time finding people powering their stuff this way! And still useful and relevant today! Thanks RMS!

author: @g7mks383
text: Good project. I remember on ebay a while ago. A shower radio run from the in line hose to the shower head. That worked on the same principle to run the radio.

author: @sunsandsoil
text: Amazing Rob. You just gave me an idea. I was in a squatter camp/ informal settlement yesterday here in South Africa. They have water taps for people to access water, and there is nearly always a line of people filling 25lt drums. If you place one or a series of these on each tap, they could still access water, but also generate some power. How much would this this generate and could we have a enough to charge a few cell cell phones or rechargeable lights while they wait?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that's an awesome idea mate - depending on the build these are capable of some generation so I would think - yes they would do that

author: @stevenfaber3896
text: Forgive me but why does it need to be on an angle to the surface plane, if you could somehow run the jet tangentially to the edge of the Inner disc? Could you please explain? the polarity of the water molecule should still allow it to stick and then pull away when others are present...right? also can we drill holes and scale it?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yeah that would work it's just easier to build it so it hits the front but you are right the edge would be good too

author: @paulmaydaynight9925
text:
so... a 1000 to 2000 rpm variable capacitor disks with forced rain water [trompe pressure driven] direct drive &/or slower back driven thingy verse Cycloidal Drive gear to flywheel painted disk generators..
just because, add in spinning magnetic induction in the metal case to make induction heating of some of the water

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: now there are some interesting combinations mate

author: @woodworks2123
text: I've looked into the history of these water motors and find them fascinating. I think you have played with the tiny ones you can buy on Amazon that can maybe generate about 5 Watts but those old water motors seemed to be able to extract a good amount of power from the water supply. ( I'm not sure if the old pressure is the same pressure that we get now)  even a measly 100 watts over a 24 hour period. 2.4kwh is about half of my daily use in a normal 3 bed house so a huge saving esp now. This, several solar panels, a small rose wind turbine, hamster wheel generator windwall fence and raindrop generator roof tiles, store the energy in a few fwg batteries and hey presto I'm a self sustaining power station. 😁

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I think a multiple approach is the answer mate

replies.author: @TimeSurfer206
replies.text:
Hybrid is the ONLY way to go, ESPECIALLY when you have Small Scale Hydro available.
That 2.4 KWh of daily production represents 16.8 KWh  of battery storage you don't need, on a 7 day storage protocol, _and that's a LOT of Battery!_

author: @David_Mash
text: I'm usually pretty savvy with this stuff but didn't really see the difference from a tesla turbine or boundary layer turbine

replies.author: @Barskor1
replies.text: Tesla turbines have smooth disks not ruff ones. ruff surfaces are a two-edged sword it lets the water grip easily to push but it also wants to stay on the disk with micro turbulences forming layers that IMO shed energy but only at high rmps is this a problem.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Barksor1 gives a nice explanation mate

author: @mrdewilliams
text:
I love it when you include the book that inspired the project.  When I can find them, I will spend the next several days reading and exploring the information available.

For anyone interested, volumes 3-7, 9, 11, 14,15,17, and 19 of "The Model Engineer and Amateur Electrician" are all available for download on Google Books.  There are other volumes available on HathiTrust, but they are for online viewing only.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that is an awesome heads up mate - I only have up to volume 12

replies.author: @mrdewilliams
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering In the later volumes, they replace amateur with practical in the book title, if you have difficulty finding  them.

author: @ArtisticImpressionsbyBobRouth
text: Rob, if you crimp the end of the water tube slightly to create a fan like spray does it increase the speed?  more surface area being hit by the spray.

replies.author: @Barskor1
replies.text: Take a wheel and push to move it on the side start at the outer edge and then work inward as you get to the center hub it gets harder to push the wheel at the same speed as when you were at the edge.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice tip - no pun intended lol
